  • DPA with CDI Adapter is required for real time aquisition
  • Audit Logs for a specific model can be aquired in Datasphere via CDI connection type
  • Performance of remote table is crucial - consider aquiring only the latest changes via remote table and persist older changes via data flow in Datasphere. These considerations heavily depend of your requirements, model and data volume.
  • Enforcing a dynamic filter push down via remote table on the Audit_Time field was only possible by adding bind_as_value function in the where clause: Query Parameterization: BIND_AS_PARAMETER and BIND_AS_VALUE | SAP Help Portal -> when working with remote tables always check out the actual statement that's being pushed down
  • If you don't have seamless planning yet, the audit data could be loaded via import job to SAC instead of using a live version - this would require the execution of the import job to have a look at the latest data changes
  • Latency of the view on the remote table is about 3 seconds also for empty audit tables - i guess, that's the communication overhead. Good news is that a view with filter push down on huge audit tables has a similar performance

Analyzing the data in SAC

First, I will show you a simple story for planning CostCenter - GLAccount combinations. As you can see, CostCenter A and C have a total of 200 each:

When I look at the audit log story, I can see that the sum of the delta values equals the result in the plan story:

Every dimension is available as filter criteria. For consistency reasons the "SourceVersion" (i.e. the actual version in the plan model) is a single value filter. The "Version" of the audit model is the version "live". That is, because the live connection from Datasphere to SAC is established via an own version. Furthermore, in contrast to the standard application for data changes, we have now the ability to use the new "Quick Builder" in the story to drag and drop dimensions to further analyze the data changes. We could also apply hierarchies to answer business related questions like: "Why and when did my EBIT change?"

Undo data changes / recover plan data in SAC

A critical incident would be the unintended deletion of the whole version data. In this small example I deleted the "public.Budget" data for the whole model. In the audit story the deletions are visible:

Since the audit data is in a SAC model now, the delta records can be used for a cross model copy step to undo this data change. One way to achieve it is by appending the inverted delta value in the cross model copy step. In this example model I build a cross model copy step with all possible filter criteria as parameters (also, for instance, the audit time). Those input parameters are bound to the story filters in this specific story. So, by filtering to the specific audit date in this example, the data action ("Undo Selection") - inheriting the cross model copy step - is prefilled:

After execution of the data action and reset of the story filter, the inverted delta bookings can be seen in the audit log story:

Also in the plan story, the data is visible again!

Remarks regarding cross model copy step:

  • The relevant dimensions (here: CostCenter, GLAccount) should be public in both models
  • The date dimension, by its private nature, should be generated identically in the audit model as in the plan model
  • Theoretically, fact and corresponding master data could be deleted in the plan model as part of a housekeeping task. Keep in mind that the audit logs should be deleted for those combinations aswell to avoid warning messages.
  • If you check out the data action prompt, you can see that the "SourceVersion" and the "TargetVersion" (i.e. the version in the plan model) are two different prompts. It probably never makes sence to recover data to another "TargetVersion" as the "SourceVersion". To eliminate potential risks here, the "TargetVersion" prompt could be filled via script.

Towards a Reliable Change‑Recovery Process in SAC

SAC’s open, self‑service-oriented planning allows users to adjust productive data directly, which speeds up planning but also introduces a risk to data quality, especially when self‑service stories are used in production. In our project, this led to situations where entire versions were deleted unintentionally or values were spread across all dates due to a single user action. Recovery required manual corrections based on audit logs or deletion of unintended data.

Live Versions improve the situation by capturing delta values and deletions more consistently and reducing gaps that previously occurred through import jobs. This strengthens the audit trail and provides a more reliable basis for analyzing changes and restoring data. However, the audit log is not guaranteed to remain perfectly synchronized with the planning model (entries may be missing or deleted), so it should always be viewed as a time‑limited recovery source.

The approach described in this blog uses audit data in Datasphere and SAC to make changes analyzable across dimensions and to restore data selectively using inverted deltas. This creates a practical, repeatable recovery mechanism that reduces the risks of self‑service planning in production while preserving SAC’s flexibility. It covers not only new and modified records but also deleted ones.

And for anyone at SAP working on seamless planning: the recent developments are very encouraging! Making audit logs natively accessible in Datasphere would further strengthen performance and simplify recovery scenarios.

entry.title: Blog 2 - Accelerating SAP BW Modernization: Hands on Experiences with the BDC Data Product Generator
entry.content:
We explored in part 1 of this blog series the upcoming 2027 End of Maintenance deadline for NW BW 7.5 customers and what they can do next, based on practical experiences in a recent pilot project. This follow-on blog explores once the BW system is ‘lifted’ into SAP Business Data Cloud PCE, thereby extending the maintenance of the as-is environment) what enhanced capabilities there are for customers to kick start their system modernization.

As a recap, the Lift phase in BW to BDC refers to customers migrating their existing SAP BW or SAP BW/4HANA on-premises deployment as-is to the private cloud component of SAP Business Data Cloud, without requiring system redesign or data model changes.

Once a customer’s as-is BW is available in PCE as a part of BDC, the steps to modernize can begin. It is well documented that SAP BDC lays the foundation of the Data Product economy for the modern SAP Business Suite, but BDC also is responsible for Data Products created from SAP BW data. The creation of local tables (files) in the object store of SAP Datasphere in BDC is orchestrated via the ‘Data Product Generator’ which can be installed on BW once it is available as a part of the BDC ecosystem in PCE. This is applicable for BW7.5 on HANA and/or BW/4HANA.

The BW Data Product Generator (BW DPG) is a tool that enables SAP BW customers to easily replicate InfoProvider data to the managed object store (HDLFS) of SAP Datasphere within SAP Business Data Cloud. It transforms traditional BW data into modern data products for consumption in analytical scenarios and AI/ML workflows. An informative introductory blog on the DPG can be found here

For our complex BW pilot case, we used the BW DPG to generate object store local tables (files) which we then used either in SAP Datasphere for mixed BW modelling and analytical scenarios, or as a base for creating data products making them available in SAP Databricks via delta share (zero copy).

In the SAP Gui using transaction code RSDPS, the Data Product Generator lists the subscriptions both active and inactive.

The Data Product Generator for users on the Eclipse modelling environment is also available

A BW DPG subscription is a configuration object in the SAP BW Data Product Generator that defines how data from specific BW InfoProviders should be replicated to SAP Datasphere’s Object Store within SAP Business Data Cloud. It serves as a replication package that specifies which InfoProviders to extract, what data fields to include, and how the replication should be executed (Initial only or Initial with Delta).

Our experience with the data product generator in the pilot project showcased that even in v1 it has great capabilities to access any InfoProvider in SAP BW7.5 and load into the BDC custom file store in HDLFS. There were optimizations to be made on the time to activate a subscription, and many enhancements have just been delivered, plus more are on the way in the near term roadmap for the DPG.

Screenshot of v2 of the Data Product Generator 

Once the Data Products were created from the BW Info Provider data in Datasphere (via the data sharing cockpit), it was very simple to drag and drop them into the canvas for building data models/analytical data sets. Enhancements around association handling (mentioned below) will maintain relationships from the source objects, enabling faster and more complete modelling of the BW Data Products in SAP BDC Datasphere.

 Of course, these data products are available at this stage to be Zero Copy Data shared to SAP Databricks or via BDC Connect to certified targets.

Data Product Generator Roadmap

Certain features were not available at the time of our pilot, but are now released, that would have made a big impact on the customer use case. Important items amongst others are mass object handling and semantic onboarding of association information from BW objects.

  • Handling of multiple subscriptions within one tooling (eclipse-based) when selecting an entity from SAP Business Warehouse (SAP BW)
  • Inclusion of association information (semantic onboarding)
  • Creation of corresponding subscriptions for dependent entities of the selected entity from SAP BW

These capabilities now being released will enable customers to easily select objects and handle through the creation of multiple subscriptions in one tooling, including information on associations where subscriptions can also be automatically created if selected. This will increase the time to value and enhance the experience for self service modelling in Datasphere, with mappings between the BW objects available automatically. Additionally, the creation of the Data products themselves via the DPG is something to look forward to in Q2 2026.

The roadmap information for DPG and other topics can be found here –

https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?range=FIRST-LAST&BC=6DF54C88F6211FD082B1933C9215B4BE#Q4%202025;INNO=0...

In summary, the Data Product Generator is handling the ‘shift’ phase of the BW modernization journey, creating local tables (files)in the HDLFS layer of BDC out of BW Info Providers, making this treasure chest of historic data (plus potentially new data changes) available for modern AI/ML and GenAI use cases.

Stay tuned to our blog series to discover more about how to transition from BW 7.5 to SAP Business Data Cloud whilst reusing Extractors Custom Logic and Models

entry.title: BW Modernisation with the SAP BW Data Product Generator
entry.content:
The DataProduct Generator for SAP Business Data Cloud in SAP BW/4HANA Administration Cockpit

With the "SAP BW/4HANA DataProduct Generator for SAP BDC" a functionality is availabe as part of the BW/4HANA Administration Cockpit to expose existing SAP BW/4HANA artifacts ( i.e. Advanced DataStore Objects, CompositeProviders and Queries as an InfoProvider) as local tables to the Object Store. For SAP BW 7.5 there is a SAP GUI based UI available. In a second step the Data Sharing Cockpit of Datasphere can be used to create DataProducts out of such local tables.

1) Activities in the Data Provisioning Cockpit:

Scenario: The underlying Scenario of this chapter is to create a DataProduct out of an Advanced DataStore Object from a SAP BW/4HANA system in the private Cloud which containes additional CashFlow data. This data should be made available as a DataProduct to BDC to be merged with the existing Working Capital data from the exercises before.

Update March 2026: Since the availability of SAP BW/4HANA Modeling Tools in Eclipse 1.27 there is an additional way to move a BW/4HANA InfoProvider to the Object Store of SAP Datasphere (as a basis for the Data Product generation). As a result, a subscription is generated. A data subscription is a new BW object type that allows you to extract data from ODP-based sources within your BW system into local tables (file) in the SAP Datasphere system of your SAP Business Data Cloud formation. In the following chapters both options are described:

1.1) Exposing an InfoProvider of SAP BW/4HANA via the Data Product Generator (in SAP BW/4HANA Administration Cockpit)

  • Establish a subscription in SAP BW/4HANA Administration Cockpit, select the InfoProvider which needs to be exposed to the ObjectStore and activate the subscription:

(remark: "CF in thge description of the objects in the screenshots is referring to the scenario of the exercise and means "Cash Flow")

  • Regular transfers can be scheduled from the subscription using a BW Process Chain. With every execution of the BW Process Chain the data is pushed into the SAP Datasphere object store. There is a new Process Type available to be used to propagate the data of an InfoProvider to the ObjectStore "Load Process and Postprocessing - Data Subscription":

The status of the latest execution of a subscription can be checked in the subscription overview:

For each subscription the local table can be viewed as well as its content. By pressing "View Local Table" for a given subscription you will be guided to the Datasphere instance and the respective local table in the Data Builder. From here the content of the table can also be checked.

1.2) Exposing an InfoProvider of SAP BW/4HANA via the Scenario Generator (with the SAP BW/4HANA Modeling Tools in Eclipse)

With the SAP BW/4HANA Modelling Tools in Eclipse 1.27 there is an additional possibility to expose a BW InfoProvider to the Object Store of SAP Datasphere. Eitherway, regardless which option is used, the local table (subscription) has to be used as a basis in the Data Sharing Cockpit to generate a Data Product.

There are three important concepts which neeed to be considered using the Scenario Generator:

A 'scenario' is a list of references between data subscriptions which are required to reconstruct a specific BW model (such as a DataStore object (advanced) (aDSO) or a CompositeProvider (HCPR)). For example, if you have a CompositeProvider, you would not only need a data subscription to represent the top-level object, but also further data subscriptions for the InfoObjects it contains and other dependencies.

The 'scenario tree' saves these dependencies between the objects in the BW model, as well as the equivalent data subscriptions and their dependencies. It also considers existing data subscriptions for dependent objects to avoid the creation of duplicate data subscriptions and local tables (file).

The 'Scenario Generator' lets you not only create scenarios out of your BW models, but also mass generate the associated data subscriptions out of the scenario tree. Through this you can replicate an entire BW model into local tables (file) in your SAP Datasphere tenant in SAP Business Data Cloud. There, the extracted data and associated semantics are stored in table format and become available for further use cases, such as creating data products or sharing to SAP Databricks.

So the basic workflow in the Scenario Generator is:

a) To create a Scenario which is a collection of all Objects belonging to a given BW InfoProvider and their dependencies. For example a CompositeProvider alone does not contain any data. The data is coming from its PartProviders so a scenario of a CompositeProvider would also include the PartProviders.

b) Generate the subscriptions which consists of the lokal tables and the mechanisms to replicate the data from the InfoProviders to the local tables

As a basis for the Data Product to be created this time the CompositeProvider (DPG_CP_CF) from the scenario above is taken. In the Scenario Generators first tab 'Scenario Core Objects' some general settings can be made before collecting the objects related to a selected InfoProvider to create the scenario and the dependency tree of all objects belonging to the scenario.

  • 'Selection Grouping' defines which related objects shoud be collected for a selected BW InfoProvider. It can be specified that 'only necessary' objects are taken into consideration or also the Upstream and downstream objects along the Dataflow of the InfoProvider.
  • The 'Association Levels' define the amount of levels included in the dependency tree.
  • There are some optional checkboxes to activate the possibility to transport subscriptions or to consider already existing subscriptions to prevent duplicates and redundant subscriptions on objects where a subscription already exists.

After pressing the 'Collect' Button, all dependent objects from the CompositeProvider are collected which are the Inbound ADSO as the only PartProvider and the second Object collected is the InfoObject 'Company Code' which is the only real InfoObject in the Inbound ADSO (rest of the columns are fieldbased).

After the collection of the objects the relationsships are displayed in the Tab 'Object Relations'. In this list all subscriptions which are generated in this second step can be seen and the generation of the subscription triggered. After this the 'Message' tab is shown where all relevant messages from the generation process can be seen.

2) Creation of a DataProduct out of the exposed SAP BW/4HANA ADSO

To create a DataProduct from the local table the Data Sharing Studio from Datasphere must be used. In a BDC Formation there can only be one Datasphere System assigned to the BW 7.5 or BW/4HANA instance.

Before the DataProduct can be generated, a Data Provider Profile must be defined (or an existing one used) where the mandatory flag "Data Provider Visibility" is set to "Unified Customer Landscape Formations".

Now a new DataProduct can be created (Data Sharing Cockpit > My Data Products > Create Product)

In the definition of the DataProduct the fields for the Contexts and Deliverymode are automatically taken from the Data Provider Profile and to connect to the right ADSO the corresponding Datasphere Space needs to be specified where the ADSO is located:

After saving the DataProduct it should be available in the overview table under "My Data Products". By selecting this entry in the table the Status can be switched to "listed".

3) Next Steps

Create a graphical view to bring the data of this newly created DataProduct together to share it with other data in SAP Datasphere or expose the Data Product for further Analytical Scenarios (e.g. via Databricks).

 

4) Important Links / Notes

Pre-requisites

  • SAP Note [3590400 - Configuration SAP BW and SAP BW/4HANA: Data Product Generator for SAP Business Data Cloud] (https://me.sap.com/notes/3590400)

  • SAP Note [3590318 Pilot BW Configuration for Data Product Generator in SAP BDC] (https://me.sap.com/notes/3590318)

  • SAP Note [3593519, TCI2 for BW on HANA, BW/4HANA] (https://me.sap.com/notes/3593519)

  • SAP Note 3581186 - OA2C: RFC API

  • SAP Notes for Transaction SOAUTH2_CLIENT:

  • 3532137 - OA2C: Dynpro UI for OAuth2.0 Client Configuration

  • 3561719 - OA2C: New dynpro UI SOAUTH2_CLIENT, generation of DDIC object

  • 3561640 - OA2C: Token Status for SOAUTH2_CLIENT

  • 3692414 - Data Subscription TLOGO: After TCI 3.0

  • Supported Releases of SAP BW 7.5 / SAP BW/4HANA:

  • SAP BW 7.50 SP24 onwards

  • SAP BW/4HANA 2021 SP4 onwards

  • SAP BW/4HANA 2023 SP0 onwards

entry.title: I finally found a use for AI in Datasphere
entry.content:
Introduction

EDIT: Improvement request 368218 created. Please vote 🙂 

Welcome to yet another blog post where I try to close the gab between Datasphere and BW. 

In this blog, however, my main goal is to shine a spotlight on a very small, but important part of the Datasphere solution, that could be infinitely improved, if someone has 5 minutes to spare at SAP, when they're done trying to introduce AI generated semantics, or some other buzzword soup.

Today, ladies and gentlemen, we're talking about calendar translation tables - so buckle up! 
You're in for a ride.

Background

So, until recently, you might not ever have heard of Denmark. We're a small kingdom in Scandinavia, and we have our own language, but since we're home to around 6 mio. people, we grew up with subtitles in all movies and TV, and if you wanted to read the latest books, you had to order an English version, rather than wait 6 months for the translation.

So, why do I bring that up? 
Because most software - especially for public sector clients -  in Denmark has to be available in Danish as well. 

Which brings us to SAP and Datasphere.

Like most of their solutions, Datasphere is available in up to 40 languages, and even allows users to choose between dialects for large languages like Portuguese, French, Chinese  and English.

Hell, you can have the UI in one language, and the data in another - how cool is that!? 

The problem

Okay, so let's get into the issue here. One issue is the translation services, which is going to be another blog post entirely, I promise.
But for the actual data, the dimension-to-text-entity paradigme works fine - until you're missing a text in the selected data language, in which case it'll just display the key, which is also fine. In most cases you can go back to whatever source you have and create the missing texts, and ✨ tadaaaaa ✨ now the texts are there. 

Except for one case . The time dimensions. 

Datasphere allows you to, on a given space, generate time dimension data, which you can then use to model your time dimensions. 
When you do that, you also get three translation tables, Day, Month, and Quarter, that provides the texts for all the supported data languages- right?

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Nope. 

Well, obviously we can just open the tables and add new records? 

Nope. 

I managed to find an SAP Note, which - I have to say - is pure comedy gold. 
3288674 - Translation Table Missing Languages - SAP for Me

"The translation tables are automatically created by SAP Datapshere for the EN, ES, FR and DE languages and it is not able to edit or add more to it."

Yep, you read that right. If your user has their data access language set to anything except English, French, Spanish, or German - you just get the keys. 

Okay, what the hell. Guess I'll just copy the tables, add my own values and union them in a view - bit of a pain in the backside, but I can do that at least, right?

You guessed it - nope. You can't copy it. 

So, if you are from a small kingdom in Scandinavia, working for a local public sector client, you have to create it all on your own. 

So why only those four languages?

And okay, I admit, that's not too much work - will take you half an hour at most to get everything sorted out. 
Why, oh why Christian are you so upset?

Surely SAP has very good, logical, technically complex reason why they only support those four languages, right? 

See, that's what I thought as well. I had several theories, including the locales installed on the OS the server was running on.

But no. No no no. I'll show you. And I'll show you how to check that I'm not making it up. Because it sounds made up. 

So, first of all to replicate the solution, you have to create a DB-user on your Datasphere tenant, like described in this blog post, and then open the Database Explorer:

From there, open the Catalog, selection functions, limit the results to the "DWC_GLOBAL" schema and search for "GENERATE". 

Open up any of the _LOOKUP functions, and select the "CREATE" statement tab. 

And now you see. 

 

It's just a hardcoded list. 
The reason you have to spend your valuable time on this earth create copies of tables and uploading your values, is because someone only bothered to hardcode four languages. 

Does that not raise your pulse a bit? 

Just wait until I tell you that this problem was already solved.
In all SAP system, you can find some standard tables. For months, open any SAP system and access table T247. It's all in there. 
It has been a solved problem for decades. 

Solution - Let's nuke the fridge with some AI, and some sarcasm.

Okay, so maybe the SAP developer didn't have access to an SAP system so check those tables. 
Or maybe it was just too much work to format the values into SQL. 
There could be many reasons. None of them are good. 

But okay, let's do all the cool kids, and not spend ½ an hour to format the SQL query, let's waste some water with AI.

So I basically just copy-pasted the SQL function, told it to add all the languages in the documentation, and ignore the ones in there. 
Oh, and I just used the Co-pilot embedded in my MSTeams client.

So, here you go SAP, have it all! 

-- FILEOWNER: [Provisioning, Spaces]
CREATE FUNCTION "DWC_GLOBAL"."_DIM_GENERATE_MONTH_LOOKUP"
(
  IN IV_LANGUAGE NVARCHAR(10)   -- <== enlarged from 5
)
RETURNS TABLE (
  "ID" NVARCHAR(2),
  "_DESCRIPTION" NVARCHAR(50),
  "_LANGU" NVARCHAR(10)         -- <== enlarged from 5
)
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY INVOKER AS
BEGIN
  DECLARE months TABLE("ID" NVARCHAR(2), "_DESCRIPTION" NVARCHAR(50), "_LANGU" NVARCHAR(10));

  -- EXISTING DATA (en, de, fr, es) ... keep your current inserts here
  -- ----------------------------------------------------------------
  -- [Your original EN/DE/FR/ES inserts not repeated for brevity]

  -------------------------------------------------------------------
  -- NEW LOCALES BELOW (CLDR "abbreviated" month names unless CLDR uses numeric+unit)
  -------------------------------------------------------------------

  -- bg-BG (Bulgarian)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','ян.','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','февр.','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','март','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','апр.','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','май','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','юни','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','юли','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','авг.','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','септ.','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','окт.','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','ноем.','bg-BG'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','дек.','bg-BG'));

  -- ca-ES (Català)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','gen.','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','febr.','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','març','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','abr.','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','maig','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','juny','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','jul.','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','ag.','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','set.','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','oct.','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','nov.','ca-ES'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','des.','ca-ES'));

  -- zh-CN (Chinese, Simplified) – CLDR uses numeric + 月
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','1月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','2月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','3月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','4月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','5月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','6月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','7月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','8月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','9月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','10月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','11月','zh-CN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','12月','zh-CN'));

  -- zh-TW (Chinese, Traditional) – same pattern
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','1月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','2月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','3月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','4月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','5月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','6月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','7月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','8月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','9月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','10月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','11月','zh-TW'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','12月','zh-TW'));

  -- hr-HR (Croatian)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','sij','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','velj','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','ožu','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','tra','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','svi','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','lip','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','srp','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','kol','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','ruj','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','lis','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','stu','hr-HR'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','pro','hr-HR'));

  -- cs-CZ (Czech)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','led','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','úno','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','bře','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','dub','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','kvě','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','čer','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','čvc','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','srp','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','zář','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','říj','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','lis','cs-CZ'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','pro','cs-CZ'));

  -- da-DK (Danish)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','jan.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','feb.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','mar.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','apr.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','maj','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','jun.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','jul.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','aug.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','sep.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','okt.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','nov.','da-DK'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','dec.','da-DK'));

  -- nl-NL (Dutch - Netherlands)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','jan.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','feb.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','mrt.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','apr.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','mei','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','jun.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','jul.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','aug.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','sep.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','okt.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','nov.','nl-NL'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','dec.','nl-NL'));

  -- et-EE (Estonian)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','jaan','et-EE'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','veebr','et-EE'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','märts','et-EE'));

  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','okt','et-EE'));

  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','dets','et-EE'));

  -- fi-FI (Finnish) – CLDR short forms with periods
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','tammik.','fi-FI'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','helmik.','fi-FI'));

  -- el-GR (Greek)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','Ιαν','el-GR'));

  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','Δεκ','el-GR'));

  -- hi-IN (Hindi) – CLDR abbreviated (uses “॰” for many months)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','removed from blogpost, because it gave an error :(','hi-IN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','removed from blogpost, because it gave an error :(','hi-IN'));

  -- hu-HU (Hungarian)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','jan.','hu-HU'));

  -- id-ID (Indonesian)

  -- it-IT (Italian)
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','gen','it-IT'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','feb','it-IT'));

  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','thg 2','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','thg 3','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','thg 4','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','thg 5','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','thg 6','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','thg 7','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','thg 8','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','thg 9','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','thg 10','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','thg 11','vi-VN'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','thg 12','vi-VN'));

  -- cy-GB (Welsh) – CLDR-derived abbreviations
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('01','Ion','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('02','Chwef','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('03','Maw','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('04','Ebrill','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('05','Mai','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('06','Meh','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('07','Gorff','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('08','Awst','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('09','Medi','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('10','Hyd','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('11','Tach','cy-GB'));
  :months.("ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU").insert(('12','Rhag','cy-GB'));

  -- Return one language or all
  IF :IV_LANGUAGE IS NOT NULL THEN
    -- If provided language not available, fall back to EN
    SELECT DISTINCT "_LANGU" INTO IV_LANGUAGE DEFAULT 'en'
    FROM :months
    WHERE "_LANGU" = :IV_LANGUAGE;

    RETURN
      SELECT "ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU"
      FROM :months
      WHERE "_LANGU" = :IV_LANGUAGE;
  ELSE
    RETURN SELECT "ID","_DESCRIPTION","_LANGU" FROM :months;
  END IF;
END;

A cutting-edge AI code example, ready for your Datasphere solution! 
Please, can I have my allstar badge now? Can I open a pull request somewhere? 

Or maybe, maybe, we could not hardcode the bloody thing?

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in February
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in February

February depression—no way!
Plenty of great features and enhancements being delivered with our two releases in SAP Datasphere and BDC, and SAP Analytics Cloud also delivered a packed quarterly release.

Learn more in my short top features video in February 2026 on YouTube 📺 as well as the QRC1 2026 release for SAP Analytics Cloud by @Orla_C .

In addition, explore the latest updates in our community news blogs and more.

 

My top features in February

 

Data Pipeline Analyzer

The Data Pipeline Analyzer is a monitoring tool designed for real-time analysis of complex data models, providing immediate visibility into errors, warnings, and data discrepancies. By focusing on node status indicators and streamlined navigation, the tool allows users to pinpoint the exact location of data transfer failures. This targeted troubleshooting approach ensures efficient oversight of flows, enabling organizations to maintain high data quality and resolve issues promptly to prevent downstream disruptions.

➡️  More information.

 

Task Chain Output Parameter

After introducing input parameters already in Q3 last year, you can now use output parameters in task chains. You can also map output parameters to the task chain from the task object. This will allow for more flexible usage of task chains and nested task chains.

➡️  More information.

 

Transformation Flow Object Versioning

The versioning concept you know for other modeling objects is now available for transformation flows. You can now review and restore versions, and the general options are similar to other editors where versioning is already available. You can:

  • Open a past version in read-only mode.
  • Download a past version to a CSN/JSON file.
  • Restore a past version to replace the current version.

➡️  More information.

 

Local Table (Files) Delete Inbound Buffer

Local Tables (File) store a large amount of data, including delta capture changes and historical data. When data is updated, the information is kept on your local table (file) with the delta changes columns, and a new version is stored in the object store. Keeping all these records and versions can consume a lot of memory, and can impact performance.
You can now delete records waiting in the inbound buffer of a local table (file), even if a merge task has not run.

➡️  More information.

 

Visibility of Data Access Controls Applied to Sources

A small but helpful feature, where you can now see data access controls applied to the sources of your view in a subsection Applied via Sources under the list of Data Access Controls in the side panel.

➡️  More information.

 

Sharing of Analytic Models

We all waited a long time for the cross-space sharing of an analytic model together with its contributing entities like the fact, dimensions, hierarchies, and text entities. You can share an analytic model with one or even with multiple spaces.

This enables users in the target space with the ability to stack on the model, inheriting its properties and enhancing the new model to meet their needs.

➡️  More information.

 

 

SAP Analytics Cloud Feature Highlights QRC1

The first release for SAP Analytics Cloud for 2026 is already out and @orla Cullen dives a little deeper into the top 5 features of the first quarter:

🔹New Chart Types – Expanded native visualizations with Area and Pareto charts to simplify advanced analysis.

🔹Story Versioning – Create, manage, and restore up to 10 major story versions for better iterative control.

🔹Comment Management – A new dashboard to search, filter, copy, and bulk-delete comments across models and versions.

🔹My Metrics Alerting – Subscribe to metric reports, schedule delivery, and get in-app/email notifications when metrics change.

🔹Live Connectivity to Snowflake – Extended external live data access for planning models connected directly to Snowflake.

 

 

 

Webinar Series: The Next Era of Business Data

SAP Business Data Cloud is part of a comprehensive strategy for enterprise data designed to address complex enterprise data management challenges. We provide an overview of SAP Business Data Cloud and guide you through its integral pillars and strategic vision.

👉 Sign up now: SAP BDC: The Next Era of Business Data

🔹Jan 29 | Discover SAP Business Data Cloud in 2026 - recording

🔹Feb 05 | The Smart Path to SAP BW Modernization - recording

🔹Feb 19 | Empowering SAP Datasphere Users with SAP Business Data Cloud Innovations - recording

🔹Feb 26 | From Data to Decisions: Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP BDC

🔹Mar 12 | From Data Silos to Business Insight: The Power of BDC Connect

🔹Mar 19 | An Architect's Blueprint for SAP Business Data Cloud as the Foundation for Enterprise AI

🔹Mar 26 | Design. Govern. Deliver. Data Products - Data Product Studio

🔹Apr 16 | Breaking Down the SAP BDC Pricing Model

 

More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

entry.title: SAP ORD Made Simple: What It Does and Why It’s Crucial in your SAP Landscape + Purpose and Value
entry.content:
ORD (Open Resource Discovery) is a protocol and metadata specification developed  to enable standardized discovery and integration of business services, APIs, events, and other resources across distributed systems across SAP Products.


Find out more details on ORD : https://open-resource-discovery.org/introduction
Let’s break down what ORD is, why we created it, where it fits into your architecture, and how it adds value across your SAP landscape.

What Is SAP ORD (Open Resource Discovery)?

Open Resource Discovery (ORD) is a standardized protocol for metadata discovery that allows SAP applications and services to self‑describe their APIs, events, entity types, and other exposed resources. It provides a consistent, uniform structure for documentation, discovery, versioning, taxonomy, and lifecycle metadata.

Where ORD is Used in SAP

  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP): Core component for service discovery and consumption
  • SAP S/4HANA Public & Private Cloud: Extensions and APIs are discoverable via ORD
  • SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) : Consumer & Publisher of ORD Metadata that requires in Data Products
  • SAP Integration Suite: Uses ORD for automatic API discovery
  • SAP Graph: Built on top of ORD for unified data access

ORD essentially acts as a "catalog of catalogs," making SAP’s complex ecosystem more navigable and integration-friendly.

How to Know If an SAP API Supports ORD (Open Resource Discovery)

One of the simplest and most reliable ways to verify whether an API supports Open Resource Discovery (ORD) is to check for the ORD ID directly on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com).

When an API is published with ORD metadata from each SAP Product, automatically displays an “ORD ID” in the API details pane. This confirms that the API is fully enabled for the standardized metadata structure provided by ORD, meaning it can be seamlessly discovered, crawled, and consumed by systems such as SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC).

Where to Find the ORD ID (Screenshot Provided)

In the API detail screen — typically under Overview — you will see a section that lists:

  • Direction (Inbound / Outbound)
  • ORD ID (a unique standardized identifier representing the ORD resource)

In the example image provided, the lower-right information box contains:

  • Direction: Inbound
  • ORD ID: sap.s4:apiResource...


This clearly indicates that:

The API is published with ORD metadata and is ready for integration with SAP Business Data Cloud and its Data Products framework.

Why This Matters

Having an ORD-enabled API means:

  • Your integration can rely on standardized metadata such as entity types, relations, events, and API capabilities (as defined in SAP’s ORD specification).
  • SAP Business Data Cloud can easily discover and interpret the API’s metadata for creating or connecting to Data Products.
  • You avoid custom mapping or manual documentation because ORD ensures consistent structure and discoverability across SAP systems.

This is part of the broader SAP strategy — documented through the Open Resource Discovery protocol — to harmonize how APIs and services describe themselves across the SAP ecosystem.

Why ORD is Important in SAP

  1. Unified Integration: ORD provides a single, standardized way to describe and discover all integration resources (APIs, events, entity types) across SAP’s diverse product portfolio

  2. Automation: It enables automated discovery and consumption of services without manual documentation or configuration, significantly reducing integration effort

  3. Metadata Consistency: ORD ensures consistent metadata across systems, making it easier for developers and integration tools to understand and consume services

  4. Decentralization: Each application can expose its own ORD document describing its capabilities, eliminating the need for central catalogs that quickly become outdated

  5. Ecosystem Enablement: Partners, customers, and third-party tools can automatically discover what integration points are available in their SAP landscape

SAP Provides an Open‑Source ORD Repository for CAP Developers

To accelerate adoption of Open Resource Discovery (ORD) across custom applications, SAP also provides an open‑source GitHub repository that enables developers to implement ORD using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). This repository offers reference implementations, examples, and tooling support so developers, partners and customers can easily expose ORD‑compliant metadata from their own services.


https://github.com/cap-js/ord
 
Demo Application provides ORD

This makes it straightforward for CAP‑based applications to:

  • Self‑describe APIs, events, business objects, and data products
  • Register and publish metadata consistently with SAP’s ORD standard
  • Integrate cleanly into the broader SAP ecosystem, including SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere, SAP Graph, and SAP BTP services

Conclusion

SAP ORD (Open Resource Discovery) is one of the most important but often overlooked components in SAP’s modern integration strategy. By standardizing metadata discovery, enabling automated publication, and providing a consistent structure across SAP products, ORD delivers massive value for architects, developers, and business teams.

If you’re working with SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere, SAP Databricks or any other SAP landscapes, understanding and leveraging ORD is no longer optional—it’s essential.

entry.title: Where ORD Is Used in SAP Datasphere, SAP Business Data Cloud & SAP Databricks
entry.content:
SAP Open Resource Discovery (ORD) is becoming a foundational standard across the SAP ecosystem because it provides a consistent, self‑describing metadata layer for APIs, events, business objects, and data products. This metadata standardization is what enables smooth interoperability across SAP’s modern data and integration platforms.

Below are the key places where ORD is used today — and how it empowers seamless integration, especially with SAP Datasphere, SAP Business Data Cloud, and SAP Databricks.

1. SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com)

SAP Business Accelerator Hub uses ORD as one of the primary mechanisms for publishing APIs and events. Any API showing an ORD ID on its overview page is officially ORD-enabled, meaning it exposes standardized metadata that can be consumed by SAP tools and data platforms.

This ensures:

  • Consistent API descriptions
  • Discoverability by SAP’s central aggregators
  • Compatibility with downstream systems like SAP Business Data Cloud
This is the first indicator that an API can participate in the broader data product ecosystem. 
 

2. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)

SAP uses ORD internally to improve SAP-to-SAP integration and provide a unified metadata layer that development tools (like SAP Business Application Studio) can consume for enhanced API discovery and automation.

Each Product Owner from SAP Products LoB will be develop, design ORD to make it available in SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com) and Data Products Marketplace for you..

3. SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)

SAP Business Data Cloud actively uses ORD to describe Data Products and make them discoverable to consumers across the SAP ecosystem, including SAP Datasphere. ORD helps BDC deliver a unified catalog with:

  • Descriptions of datasets
  • Relationships
  • Capabilities
  • Higher-level metadata structures like data products and business entities
In addition, third‑party systems such as SAP Databricks are required to describe data products using ORD before publishing them into SAP Business Data Cloud.
 
This ensures smooth catalog integration and consistent metadata across SAP and non-SAP data sources.
 

4. SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere consumes data products published to SAP Business Data Cloud — and because BDC requires ORD‑based metadata, Datasphere gains:

  • Automatically discoverable data products
  • Standardized metadata for modeling
  • Compatibility with external data such as Databricks Delta Lake

Datasphere uses ORD indirectly because it relies on BDC’s ORD-driven catalog for understanding and federating data assets.

Additionally, SAP’s hybrid integration patterns show that Datasphere can integrate with Databricks using JDBC or federated models — and ORD ensures data products between these systems are consistently described.

5. SAP Databricks (Enterprise Databricks Integration)

SAP Databricks integrates deeply with SAP Business Data Cloud using BDC Connect, enabling bi-directional sharing of data products. To publish a data product from SAP Databricks to SAP Business Data Cloud:

  • The metadata must be described using ORD and CSN (Core Schema Notation)
  • The data product becomes discoverable to SAP systems (e.g., Datasphere) via BDC

Furthermore, Databricks–SAP integration uses Delta Share, JDBC, and federated models, but ORD plays the critical role of standardizing data product metadata.

This creates a smooth, unified integration path:

SAP applications → ORD-based metadata → Business Data Cloud → Datasphere & Databricks

SAP Architecture Center also confirms this hybrid pattern where business data from SAP and non‑SAP (Databricks) is harmonized through Business Data Cloud and Datasphere, enabling interoperable AI/ML and analytics scenarios.

 Refer my SAP blog

6. Data Products Across the SAP Ecosystem

ORD also provides standardized metadata for:

  • Entity Types / Business Objects
  • APIs and Events
  • Data Products 
This consistency is the key enabler for cross‑platform interoperability — especially as Data Products become central to the SAP ecosystem via Business Data Cloud and Datasphere.

Summary: How ORD Enables Smooth Integration Across SAP & Databricks

With ORD in place:

  • APIs with an ORD ID on api.sap.com can be safely consumed to build data products in SAP Business Data Cloud.
  • Business Data Cloud uses ORD metadata to catalog and serve data products to SAP Datasphere and SAP Databricks.
  • SAP Databricks uses ORD when publishing its own data products back into Business Data Cloud.
  • SAP Datasphere leverages ORD-driven metadata to discover, model, and federate data products seamlessly.

ORD is the metadata “glue” that ensures smooth integration across:

SAP BTP → Business Accelerator Hub → Business Data Cloud → Datasphere → Databricks

And the more SAP and partner solutions adopt ORD, the smoother, more automated, and more scalable integrations become across the SAP landscape.

entry.title: Blog Series: Agentic Reporting - The Next Evolution of SAP Reporting
entry.content:
Most enterprises still operate in a world of dashboards that simply report what happened. KPIs move, charts update, and alerts fire when thresholds are crossed — yet none of this tells us whether the deviation is expected, normal for this period, or a real operational risk.

With AI, reporting can evolve from passive visualization to active, context‑aware decisioning.
Instead of waiting for users to interpret numbers, the system itself can observe, understand context, reason over patterns, decide significance, and take action.

This is Agentic Reporting — where insights behave like a junior analyst embedded inside your SAP landscape (S/4HANA, SAC, Datasphere, BTP), continuously interpreting changes and surfacing only what truly needs attention.

What Makes Reporting Agentic? 

Traditional reporting is static: 

  • Predefined KPIs 
  • Rigid thresholds 
  • IF → THEN rules 
  • No learning  

Agentic reporting is dynamic: 

  •  Observes data continuously 
  • Considers context — seasonality, patterns, user role, business calendar 
  •  Determines if a deviation is meaningful 
  • Recommends or triggers action 
  • Learns from outcomes  

Why Agents > Classic Alerts/Rigid Triggers 

Reporting Triggers: 

  • Static 
  • One KPI - One Action
  • Same action every time 
  • Push Only
  • No Context, No learning  

AI Agents: 

  • Adaptive, context-aware thresholds 
  • Understands correlations across KPIs 
  • Chooses the optimal action 
  • Learns over time 
  • Proactive + conversational 

 

Agentic Reporting Use Case: KPI Breach Monitoring

Use Case: KPI Breach Monitoring Tool

Traditional Approach: 

  • “If DSO > 60 days → Send email.” 
  • No context → many false alarms. 

Agentic Approach: 

  • “Is this deviation abnormal given today’s reality?” 

 Scenario A – 8% DSO Increase (No Alert) 

Agent checks context

  • It’s the end of quarter (seasonal spike expected)
  • A major customer’s payment cycle always delays during this period
  • The same pattern occurred in the last 3 quarters

Conclusion: The deviation is expected and harmless → No alert raised.

 Scenario B – Same 8% Increase Next Month (Alert!) 

Agent checks context

  • No end of quarter ❌
  • No seasonal explanation ❌
  • pattern does not match past 3 quarters ❌
  • Impacts cashflow ❌
  • Unusual vs history ❌

Conclusion: The deviation is unexpected and risky → Escalate with analysis

 Agent provides: 

  • Root-cause patterns ✔️
  • Customer contribution ✔️
  • Impact on forecast ✔️
  • Recommended actions ✔️

 The Bottom Line 

Agentic Reporting takes organizations from: 

Dashboards you look at → Dashboards that look out for you. 

 As SAP deepens Joule, AI, and agentic capabilities, this shift becomes not only possible — but necessary.

entry.title: SAML Based Single Sign-On Setup with SAP Identity Authentication Service
entry.content:
Introduction

About SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere is SAP’s unified data service for integrating, modeling and governing data across hybrid and cloud environments. As part of SAP’s Business Technology Platform (BTP), it supports enterprise-scale analytics, governance and collaboration.

 

Need for SSO

Traditional password-based authentication poses risks such as credential sprawl and inconsistent access control. Implementing SSO in Datasphere enhances:

  •              Security: Centralized identity and access management.
  •              User Experience: Seamless access without repetitive logins.
  •              Compliance: Alignment with enterprise security and governance standards.
  •              Efficiency: Reduced administrative overhead for user provisioning and password resets.

 

Architecture Overview

SSO in SAP Datasphere is typically implemented using SAML 2.0 (Security Assertion Markup Language) through SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS).

Components Involved

  • SAP Datasphere Tenant: The target system for SSO-enabled access.
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services: Identity Authentication (IAS): Acts as the trusted authentication broker.
  • Corporate Identity Provider (IdP): Provides user authentication.
  • SAP BTP Subaccount: Links Datasphere to IAS through service bindings.

 

Authentication Flow

 

Prerequisites

Before enabling SSO, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  1. SAP Datasphere Tenant - Provisioned and accessible via SAP BTP
  2. SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) - Tenant available and linked to BTP subaccount
  3. Corporate Identity Provider - Configured to support SAML 2.0
  4. Admin Access - Administrator roles in Datasphere, IAS and IdP
  5. Email Domain - Verified in IAS for trusted domain mapping

You must have the system owner role for your SAP Datasphere tenant to perform this configuration.

 

Configuration Steps

Step 1: Access IAS Tenant

  • Log in to your SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication admin console.
  • Under Applications & Resources → Applications, create a new application named “SAP Datasphere Test”.

Step 2: Download Metadata

  • Login to Datasphere tenant: System -> Administration -> Security
  • Authentication Method: SAML Single Sign On (SSO)
  • SAML Single Sign On (SSO) Configuration: Step1 - Download Service Provider Metadata

Step 3: Configure SAML Settings in IAS

  • SAML 2.0 Configuration: Define from Metadata -> Upload the metadata downloaded from Datasphere tenant

  • Subject Name Identifier

Source - Identity Directory and Value - Email

  • Attributes:

Maintain below values in Self-defined attributes.

For SAML attributes to be recognized it is also important that the “Groups” attribute contains the value of “sac”.

  • Conditional Authentication

Select “Default Identity Provider” - > Your IDP

“Check” the checkbox – Allow users stored in Identity Authentication service to login.

  • Download Metadata file from SAML 2.0 Configuration:

Step 4: Configure Trust in Datasphere

  1. In SAP Datasphere → System → Administration → Security Settings → Identity Provider Configuration.
  2. Upload Identity Provider Metadata -> Upload.

     3. Choose a user Attribute to map to your identity provider -> Email. It should map to User ID, Email or a custom attribute. The attribute will be used to map users from your existing SAML user list to SAP Datasphere. The selected user attribute must match the NameID (Subject Name Identifier) used in your custom SAML assertion.

4. Confirm that the mapping is working -> Verify Account

Copy the URL and open it in a private/incognito browser window to avoid reusing an existing session and ensure you are prompted for a fresh login.

Upon successful verification, the Login Credential field will be highlighted with a green border. After confirming this, select “Convert” to complete the SAML SSO configuration.

 

Step 5: Test Authentication

  1. Access SAP Datasphere via SSO-enabled URL.
  2. Verify that users are redirected to IAS and authenticated by the corporate IdP. 
  3. Ensure user attributes and roles are correctly mapped in Datasphere.

Best Practices

  • Use HTTPS for all communications between systems.
  • Enable Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the corporate IdP level.
  • Periodically rotate SAML certificates. 
  • Maintain audit logs in IAS for compliance.

Conclusion

Enabling SSO in SAP Datasphere strengthens enterprise security while enhancing usability and operational efficiency. Centralizing authentication through IAS and your corporate identity provider reduces the risk of credential misuse, simplifies onboarding, and improves user productivity. As organizations continue to adopt cloud and hybrid data platforms, a well-governed identity strategy becomes essential. This SSO implementation helps establish that foundation and supports future growth and integration initiatives.

SAP Sources: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/9f804b8efa8043539289f42f372c4862/9b26536159354aea9024a99cbbe60b4e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4477056515.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: SAP DSP – Automated Test Tool
entry.content:
Minor changes in sub-views can have huge impact on dependent Views (Model-Views/FACT-Level), Models, SAC Stories. Nothing new, but “Lernt Durch Schmerz” I found that even the approach: Test-Fail-Build-Test will not help. Full regression testing is required as unexpected dependency can cause huge data issues. As with all projects, time pressure may not allow full regression testing and a single developer, nor the Product Owner, will know the entire system / impact of the requested changes.

Testing by the end-user should be the last Option.

Development in SAC is more application oriented and as of now I am not aware of a way to fully test SAC Stories from functional and data side. Alternative would be to use SAC Excel-Addin to download snapshots of AMs and compare before and after the changes were made. For this alternative VBA scripting would be required and I am not sure if fully automated Excel / Workbook based Test can be done easily. Therefore, here is a proposal for DSP based Automated Test Tool supporting CD/CI approach.

Objects Used:

Type

Usage

Table

Snapshot table

Table View (private-like/base)

Dimension view based on the Snapshot table

Model-View (query-like/consumption)

FACT-Level View consuming the Table view

Model

Model used in the Story

FACT-Level View (query-like/consumption)

Object to be regression tested

KPI Generator View

Snapshot generator for each Object to be regression tested

Data Flow

Pushing Snapshots to Snapshot table

Task Chain

Executing the Data Flow/s

Story

Evaluating the data

 

How-To:

We have a limitation in DSP, that only Views can be used in Data Flows. Therefore only base-line Measures can be used as of now. Base-line Measures test will catch 80-90% of issues, however if this limitation is a problem, an alternative would be to use OData on Models and use Azure f.E. as base for the tool.

1. Create the Snapshot table: I used Provider (I am from BW world), Date, Time, Version, KPI and Value. I omitted Unit as I select the Unit in the KPI Generator View.

2. Define the KPIs to be regression tested: selection on these KPIs should be in the past (last year f.e.) ensuring stable data. Most critical / most used base-measures should be selected as KPIs. Even Row-Count can be considered in case master data is changed (f.e. time-dependency is introduced).

3. Create the KPI Generator View: based on the chosen KPIs simply aggregate the results with dedicated KPI naming (f.E. NetRevenue, ...).

4. Create Data Flow: which will just push the Snapshots into the Snapshots table.

5. Create Taks Chain, Model, Story: for easier consumption / evaluation of the snapshots. 

Approach on DEV System (optional):  

  1. Create Snapshots via Task Chain scheduled every morning / evening
  2. Have the developers check on their own

Approach on TST System:

  1. Create Snapshots via Task Chain run before the transport
  2. Import all objects
  3. Create Snapshots via Task Chain run after the transport
  4. Compare before and after transport Snapshots
  5. All differences must be explainable

 

Snapshot table:

Field

Usage

PROVIDER

Usage (optional)

KPI

Value

Value

 

Dimension view based on the Snapshot table:

SELECT * FROM [SNAPSHOT TABLE]

Optional. I never directly use tables. Learned that from my BW experience developing Extractors on the ECC side.

 

FACT-Level View consuming the Table view:

In our case SELECT * FROM [SNAPSHOT VIEW], but FACT-Level to be used as Model-View. Can be enhanced with Associations f.E.

 

Model used in the Story:

To be used in the Story for easier evaluation.

 

Snapshot generator for each Object to be regression tested:

In this view, the KPIs and Provider are defined. Basically the most-used / most-critical base-measures. For each object tested, one Snapshot generator view should be created. Alternatively one view could be created, but having hundreds of SQL lines is not the best approach.

Story:
